title: Why Bond Prices Fall When Rates Rise

screens.heading: The see-saw
screens.body: The most important sentence in fixed income: when interest rates rise, existing bond prices fall — and vice versa. It isn't a market mood or a rule of thumb. It falls straight out of arithmetic you already have: fixed cash flows, discounted harder, are worth less today.

screens.heading: Your 3% bond in a 5% world
screens.body: You hold a bond paying a 3% coupon. Rates rise, and new bonds from equally safe issuers now pay 5%. Who would pay full price for your 3% stream when 5% is on the shelf beside it? Nobody — until your bond's price drops far enough that a buyer earns roughly 5% on it too. The price falls until the old bond competes.

screens.heading: Price adjusts until yields match
screens.body: The market doesn't renegotiate your bond's coupon — it reprices the bond. The price moves to the level where the old bond's yield matches what the market now demands for that issuer and maturity. The coupon is a time capsule of the rate environment at issue; the price is what reconciles that capsule with today.

screens.heading: The pull to par — with caveats
screens.body: One force runs the other way. For a plain bond that runs its course, the contract repays face value at maturity — so as maturity nears, the price walks back toward par: a discounted bond drifts up, a premium bond drifts down. Rate moves swing the price around a path that lands on 100. The caveats matter, though: a callable bond may be redeemed early at a set price, and a distressed issuer may default or restructure, in which case the bond never reaches par at all. Pull to par describes the plain, performing, non-callable case.

screens.heading: Not a straight line
screens.body: The see-saw isn't linear. For a given bond, a one-point fall in yields tends to lift the price a little MORE than a one-point rise cuts it. The relationship curves. Two lessons from now that curvature gets a name — convexity — and it turns out to be worth money.
